description: Irish republican paramilitary force that sought to end British rule in Northern Ireland and create an independent socialist republic. It emerged in December 1969 from a split in the IRA and was the most active republican group during the Troubles. The IRA used guerrilla tactics and bombings, killing over 1,700 people. It declared a ceasefire in 1997 and formally ended its campaign in 2005.

country_links.evidence_quote: More than A$20,000 were sent per year to the Provisionals from supporters in Australia by the 1990s.
country_links.context: Irish Australians sent money to the Provisionals.

country_links.evidence_quote: In 1977 a shipment of arms from the PLO was seized in Antwerp, Belgium.
country_links.context: A shipment of arms from the PLO to the IRA was seized in Antwerp, Belgium.

country_links.evidence_quote: Canadian supporters did not just fundraise and import weapons, but also smuggled IRA and Sinn Féin members into the United States...
country_links.context: Canadian supporters fundraised, imported weapons, and smuggled IRA members into the United States.

country_links.evidence_quote: In 2001, three Irishmen, known as the Colombia Three, were arrested and accused of training Colombian guerrillas, the Revolutionary Armed Forces of Colombia (FARC).
country_links.context: The IRA trained Colombian guerrillas (FARC) in bomb-making techniques.

country_links.evidence_quote: In May 1996, the Federal Security Service, Russia's internal security service, accused Estonia of arms smuggling, and claimed that the IRA had bought weapons from arms dealers linked to Estonia's volunteer defence force, Kaitseliit.
country_links.context: The IRA bought weapons from arms dealers linked to Estonia's volunteer defence force.

country_links.evidence_quote: the Osnabrück mortar attack on a British Army base in Germany
country_links.context: The IRA attacked a British Army base in Germany with a mortar.

country_links.evidence_quote: The IRA initially focused on defence of Catholic areas, but it began an offensive campaign in 1970 that was aided by external sources, including Irish diaspora communities within the Anglosphere, and the Palestine Liberation Organization and Libyan leader Muammar Gaddafi.
country_links.context: The IRA was aided by Libyan leader Muammar Gaddafi, indicating a supply or support relationship.

country_links.evidence_quote: Carlin explained that '[t]here were politicians in Scotland, a lot of whom were very sympathetic to the nationalist cause...' He also noted that while much of the money was donated by supporters in Glasgow, funds also came from all over the country...
country_links.context: The IRA received funds from supporters in Scotland.

country_links.evidence_quote: In 1973 the IRA was accused by the Spanish police of providing explosives for the assassination of Spanish prime minister Luis Carrero Blanco in Madrid...
country_links.context: The IRA had links with Basque separatist group ETA, providing explosives for an assassination in Madrid.

country_links.evidence_quote: It used guerrilla tactics against the British Army and RUC in both rural and urban areas, and carried out a bombing campaign in Northern Ireland and England against military, political and economic targets, and British military targets in mainland Europe.
country_links.context: The IRA carried out a bombing campaign in England and attacked British military targets in mainland Europe.

country_links.evidence_quote: Irish Americans (both Irish immigrants and natives of Irish descent) also donated weapons and money. The financial backbone of IRA support in the United States was the Irish Northern Aid Committee (NORAID)...
country_links.context: Irish Americans donated weapons and money to the IRA via NORAID and George Harrison's gunrunning network.
